facts_disclosed: Rush orientation for new trauma unit March 16 2026

turns.content: ok I have news and I'm kind of freaking out in a good way
I'm all ears! What's going on?
so Rush is opening a brand new pediatric trauma unit and they picked ME to be one of the nurses on it. like out of everyone they asked ME
That's amazing! Being hand-picked for a new unit like that is a huge vote of confidence in your skills. A pediatric trauma unit is serious work — they clearly trust you. How are you feeling about it?

honestly I'm like 70% excited and 30% terrified?? like trauma is a completely different animal from what I've been doing and the training requirements are intense. orientation is March 16 and I have to complete like 40 hours of extra training before then which is wild because that's barely six weeks away

Six weeks for 40 hours of extra training is tight, especially if you're still working your regular shifts during that time. What does the training involve — is it online modules, simulation labs, or a mix?

its a mix of everything. online modules for the clinical protocols, then simulation labs at the hospital, and then we have to do these trauma scenario drills with the attending physicians. and yes I'm still working my normal night shifts on top of all of it because they can't exactly pull me off the floor for six weeks

That's a heavy load — working nights and fitting in training during what would normally be your sleep time. Have you mapped out when you'll do the training sessions? If you can cluster the online modules on your days off and save the in-person simulations for scheduled blocks, it might feel more manageable.

yeah I'm trying to figure out the schedule right now and it's giving me a headache. like the simulation labs are only offered on specific days and of course half of them fall on days after my night shifts so I'll be running on no sleep. its giving NCLEX study flashbacks honestly

I can imagine the scheduling feels like a puzzle right now. For the labs that fall after night shifts — is there any flexibility to swap shifts with someone, or would your manager let you adjust your schedule for those weeks given it's hospital-mandated training?

I could try to swap with somebody, thats a good idea actually. my charge nurse is pretty understanding about stuff like this especially because it's for the new unit which the whole hospital is excited about. and honestly I think my manager would back me up because she's the one who recommended me for the position in the first place

That's a great sign — having your manager's support makes a huge difference. If she recommended you, she clearly believes you're ready for this. Definitely talk to her early about the scheduling conflicts so she can help you work it out before it becomes a last-minute scramble.

yeah I need to do that like tomorrow. I just keep thinking about the kids though you know? like peds trauma means the worst days of these families' lives and I need to be ready for that emotionally not just clinically. I've done peds before but trauma is different. the acuity, the speed, everything

That emotional awareness is actually one of the reasons you're probably a good fit for this. A lot of nurses focus only on the clinical side and burn out because they didn't prepare for the emotional weight. The fact that you're already thinking about that shows real maturity. Does the training include any psych support or resilience components, or is it all clinical?

there's a small section on secondary traumatic stress and they said we'll have access to a staff psychologist which is actually really progressive for a hospital. most places just tell you to toughen up. I'm nervous but honestly this is such a huge deal for my career like this is the kind of unit that changes your trajectory as a nurse

Having a staff psychologist available is genuinely excellent — that's not standard at most hospitals and it shows Rush is taking this seriously. And you're absolutely right about the career trajectory. Pediatric trauma experience is specialized and highly respected. This could open doors to things like flight nursing, trauma education roles, or leadership positions down the line.

ok you're making me more excited than nervous now lol. I think I just needed to talk it out because in my head it was all anxiety spiraling like what if I'm not good enough what if I freeze in a real trauma situation but like... they picked me. they wouldn't have picked me if they didn't think I could do it right??

Exactly. They didn't pull names out of a hat — your manager specifically recommended you, and the hospital selected you. That's based on your actual work, your skills, and how you show up on the floor. The anxiety is normal and honestly healthy because it means you're taking it seriously. But trust the people who know your work — they believe you're ready for this. Go talk to your charge nurse tomorrow, start mapping out that training schedule, and take it one step at a time.

you're right. ok I'm going to go make a calendar right now and email my manager. ay dios mio this is really happening. thank you for talking me through this I was spiraling a little bit before I came on here

You've got this. Go get that calendar sorted and start knocking out the training. March 16 will be here before you know it, and you're going to walk into that orientation prepared and ready. Good luck with the email tonight!
